chain_of_thought: Top native frame is __memcpy, which is a libc allocator/primitive; per instructions I must look at the first application-level frame below it, which is Init_SEED in libUbikeyJni.so.

I attempted to use Ghidra MCP search_functions_by_name for Init_SEED/DecryptCertExchange/JNI bridge, but MCP server responded 404 for searches, so I cannot get decompiled code or arguments. That means I cannot do the mandated in-depth memcpy argument tracing, but I still must classify based on stack and context.

Crash is a process termination in __memcpy, called from Init_SEED -> SEED_CBC -> EncCertExchangeMessage_to_CertExchangeMessage -> DecryptCertExchange -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt, under a fuzz harness fuzz_one_input.

This pattern (crypto CBC decryption of a cert-exchange message) strongly suggests memcpy is being used to move blocks / IV / key material. Under fuzzing, an out-of-bounds read/write in memcpy is the usual cause when length or pointers are derived from attacker-controlled ciphertext/structure fields without checks.

Because this code is part of a real app library and the JNI entry point is exactly the one used in production (Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt), and no evidence is given of special harness-only behavior (like passing null/env-invalid pointers), it is reasonable that fuzz input simulates actual untrusted ciphertext or protocol messages.

In CBC and cert-message parsing implementations, memcpy is normally called with lengths derived from message headers/length fields; if those are not fully verified against overall buffer size, an attacker-controlled length will reach memcpy. That is a classic memory-safety vulnerability (CWE-787/805).

However, without decompiled code, I cannot prove whether there are thorough bounds checks or fixed-size buffers inside Init_SEED/SEED_CBC/EncCertExchangeMessage_to_CertExchangeMessage/DecryptCertExchange. The stack alone only shows that memcpy crashed, not why. It could be misused in internal-only code or require impossible key/session states, but given fuzzing was able to trigger it with arbitrary input, the realistic assumption is missing length validation.

JNI bridge jdecrypt likely takes a byte array and length from Java. Those are under attacker control when app decrypts data from network/QR/remote server or NFC token. If native code trusts length/structure of the ciphertext and then memcpy’s based on internal computed sizes without rechecking against buffer length, then a malformed input from an untrusted source could reproduce the crash on-device as a denial-of-service and potentially as an out-of-bounds read/write exploitable issue.

Given: (1) crash site is a raw memcpy in app crypto code, (2) path is reachable via real JNI bridge, (3) triggered by unconstrained fuzz input (indicating input-driven failure), but (4) I lack direct code to conclusively see absence of bounds checks, I classify this as likely vulnerability, but with moderate confidence (around 0.65). Impact: memory corruption via memcpy → high severity until proven otherwise.

Exploit details cannot be exact without Java callgraph or manifest, but I can outline a conceptual exploit via providing a crafted encrypted certificate-exchange message to whatever API uses UbikeyJni.jdecrypt (e.g., via network or file), causing an out-of-bounds access and app crash; RCE feasibility is unknown but theoretically possible.
reasons: Crash occurs inside __memcpy called from app crypto routines (Init_SEED/SEED_CBC/DecryptCertExchange) in libUbikeyJni.so, indicating a likely out-of-bounds memory access.

The failing code is reached through a real JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt, not a synthetic test-only entry, so attacker-controlled ciphertext/inputs can plausibly reach it in production.

CBC/certificate-exchange parsing code commonly derives memcpy lengths from message fields; under fuzzing such patterns usually fail when length/bounds checks are missing or incomplete, consistent with this crash.
No evidence that the crash is caused solely by unrealistic harness behavior such as passing null or incompatible buffers; instead it aligns with malformed but structurally plausible input.
recommendations: Disassemble/decompile Init_SEED, SEED_CBC, EncCertExchangeMessage_to_CertExchangeMessage, and DecryptCertExchange to locate memcpy calls and add strict bounds checks on lengths and buffer sizes before each call.
At the JNI boundary (Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt), validate input buffer length and any embedded length fields against maximum expected sizes before passing to native crypto routines.

Enable and run under ASan/UBSan on-device with structured malformed inputs to pinpoint the exact out-of-bounds access and confirm whether it is a read or write, then add targeted input validation or length clamping.
assumptions: Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt is invoked in production code paths with data influenced by external/untrusted sources (e.g., network, files, QR, NFC).
The fuzz harness models realistic usage by passing arbitrary cert-exchange messages to jdecrypt without extra constraints absent in production.
There are no comprehensive internal bounds checks fully constraining memcpy length and pointers inside the SEED/CBC/certificate-exchange functions.
limitations: Ghidra MCP search/decompile calls failed (HTTP 404), so I could not inspect the actual memcpy argument expressions or length calculations.
I could not inspect the Java implementation of UbikeyJni.jdecrypt or its callgraph, so the exact attacker control over inputs and realistic reachability remain partially inferred.
The exact nature of the memcpy failure (out-of-bounds read vs write, heap vs stack) and exploitability for code execution cannot be confirmed from the given stack trace alone.

prerequisites: Attacker can influence the ciphertext or certificate-exchange message processed by the app (e.g., network protocol, QR code, NFC, or file input).
Native library libUbikeyJni.so is used in the deployed app and the JNI method UbikeyJni.jdecrypt is reachable in that context.
No additional integrity/MAC checks or protocol-level encryption prevent delivery of malformed ciphertext to the decryption routine.
exploit_pipeline: Identify an application feature that performs decryption via UbikeyJni.jdecrypt (e.g., login, certificate exchange, smart billing token processing).
Capture or reverse engineer the expected cert-exchange message format and locate fields that influence internal length calculations for SEED_CBC/DecryptCertExchange.
Craft a ciphertext whose internal length or structure fields cause native code to compute an overly large copy length or incorrect pointer used in memcpy within Init_SEED/SEED_CBC.
Deliver the crafted ciphertext to the target feature (network request, scanned QR, imported file, etc.) so that it is passed down to UbikeyJni.jdecrypt.
Observe application crash or undefined behavior; with further tuning and memory layout control, attempt to turn the out-of-bounds access into information disclosure or code execution.

chain_of_thought: Native crash occurs in Get_DER_Child during parsing of ASN.1/DER certificate exchange messages, called from Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage → DecryptCertExchange → Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt. The fuzz harness entry is fuzz_one_input, which likely feeds arbitrary data into the jdecrypt JNI method to simulate untrusted inputs (e.g., network messages or files).

Tooling limitations (404 from search_functions_by_name) prevent direct decompilation, so I cannot inspect the exact implementation of Get_DER_Child or the intermediate functions. However, semantic naming and context strongly indicate this function walks a DER-encoded ASN.1 structure to pull out a child element (TLV parsing). Typical causes of crashes in such routines include out-of-bounds reads, invalid length handling, or mis-parsing of tag/length fields when the buffer content is malformed.

The immediate top frame is the application-level function Get_DER_Child (no allocator/sanitizer frames shown). Given it is a parser for complex structured binary data, a crash when fed fuzzed ASN.1 is very likely from a logic/memory-safety issue in its bounds checking. Such parsers often assume the input is well-formed DER; if length fields point past the end of the buffer or nested objects exceed the remaining size, and those conditions are not fully checked, an out-of-bounds read/write or invalid pointer access can result.

The call chain: Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt is a JNI bridge, which in production will be fed by app-layer data (e.g., encrypted cert exchange messages supplied by a remote peer or server). Fuzz harness uses fuzz_one_input as entry and calls jdecrypt directly, which mirrors a realistic entry point (JNI), just bypassing some app logic. There is no indication of the harness passing obviously impossible arguments like NULL env or invalid array sizes; instead it likely just varies the ASN.1 blob. That means the crash is not clearly a harness-only artifact but due to native code mishandling untrusted data.

Since Get_DER_Child operates purely on attacker-supplied DER-encoded bytes, and given the typical vulnerability patterns in ASN.1/DER parsing (CWE-787 out-of-bounds, CWE-125 out-of-bounds read, or even CWE-476 null deref from unchecked pointers), it is highly plausible the crash represents a genuine bug. Without decompiled code I cannot specify the exact primitive (OOB read vs write), but the function name and usage strongly suggest a parser walking child nodes in a sequence/set; if it trusts tag/length values without checking against overall buffer length, this provides a clear tainted-data-to-unsafe-access path.

No evidence is shown of robust length validation in Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage or DecryptCertExchange; and given standard JNI patterns, Java side typically only passes a byte[] and maybe its length, without re-parsing or deeply validating ASN.1. Thus, attacker control over DER content is preserved down to Get_DER_Child. This satisfies the vulnerability definition: untrusted data directly influences internal parsing logic leading to unsafe behavior and crash, likely exploitable at least for denial-of-service and possibly beyond.

Given the limited introspection but strong semantic cues and realistic entry path, I classify this as a likely real vulnerability in native DER/ASN.1 parsing, with medium-high confidence and at least high severity for DoS/memory corruption in cryptographic certificate handling.
reasons: Crash in Get_DER_Child, a DER/ASN.1 child-node parser, when fed fuzzed ASN.1 input
Call path from JNI jdecrypt indicates native code parses attacker-controlled certificate exchange messages without clear bounds checks
Typical failure mode of such functions is out-of-bounds access or invalid pointer dereference due to trusting ASN.1 length fields
Fuzz harness entry mirrors realistic JNI use rather than contrived harness-only misuse
recommendations: Reverse-engineer Get_DER_Child and ensure all tag/length parsing strictly checks against the total buffer size and remaining bytes
Add centralized ASN.1/DER validation and reject malformed structures before deep parsing or allocation
Introduce robust unit/fuzz tests for ASN.1 parsing routines using libFuzzer/oss-fuzz-like setups to cover edge cases
assumptions: fuzz_one_input passes arbitrary DER-like blobs into the jdecrypt JNI method as a byte array
Get_DER_Child is implemented in C/C++ and directly dereferences pointers into the ASN.1 buffer
No comprehensive ASN.1 validation is performed on the Java side before invoking native parsing
limitations: Could not decompile Get_DER_Child or related functions due to tooling errors; exact root cause (OOB read vs write vs null deref) is unknown
No concrete register/memory state at crash site was available to distinguish corruption from simple abort

crash.ProgramEntry: cktrace stopped: previous frame identical to this frame
StackTrace: cktrace stopped: previous frame identical to this frame

chain_of_thought: Native crash terminates via abort → __stack_chk_fail → Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage, all in libUbikeyJni.so.

__stack_chk_fail is raised by compiler-inserted stack canaries when a stack buffer (or adjacent data) is overwritten; this strongly implies an out-of-bounds write in Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage or in an inlined/helper it calls.
The function name Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage suggests parsing or transforming ASN.1-encoded data into an internal certificate-exchange message format.

The JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt is the only known Java→native entrypoint related to this crash and is in the same library; typical pattern is that jdecrypt receives attacker-controllable ciphertext / protocol messages (possibly from network, QR, NFC, etc.), then passes raw or minimally-checked buffers into internal parsing functions such as Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage.

There is no evidence of harness-only behavior like forced NULL pointers; instead, the crash arises from normal library logic under malformed ASN.1 data. Fuzzing merely accelerates discovery of such inputs, but a remote/attacker input could realistically reach the same native code via the app's decryption/handshake flows.

Stack canary failure means some local variable or saved return address region was corrupted: that is clear memory corruption (likely stack-based buffer overflow) rather than a benign abort; such conditions are typically exploitable for control-flow hijacking, though modern mitigations (ASLR, NX, stack canaries) may raise difficulty, not eliminate risk.

No validation layer between Java and native is visible from the crash record; UbikeyJni.jdecrypt likely takes a Java byte[] or String and length and passes them down. Unless the native layer performs robust bounds-checked ASN.1 parsing everywhere, malformed lengths or deeply nested/oversized ASN.1 structures can easily cause out-of-bounds writes when copying into fixed-size local buffers.

Given the very small native stack (directly into Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage then __stack_chk_fail), the bug is localized within that function; the abort is a consequence of compiler-inserted protection, not explicit error handling. This is a textbook memory safety vulnerability (CWE-121/787).

Reachability in the real app is plausible: UbikeyJni.jdecrypt is a public JNI method used for cryptographic operations; if any upstream data comes from untrusted network or external files, an attacker can craft ASN.1 blobs to feed it. Even if only partially attacker-controlled, the fuzz finding shows that certain inputs will corrupt memory before failing.

Therefore, classify this as a genuine, likely exploitable memory-corruption vulnerability in libUbikeyJni.so, rooted in unsafe ASN.1 parsing / buffer handling in Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage. Confidence is high due to presence of __stack_chk_fail and clear cryptographic parsing context, though exact line/snippet is unavailable.
reasons: Stack canary failure (__stack_chk_fail) in Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage indicates a stack-based buffer overflow or similar memory corruption.
The vulnerable function parses ASN.1 certificate-exchange data, which is typically derived from attacker-controllable or at least untrusted input passed through UbikeyJni.jdecrypt.
Abort is triggered by compiler-inserted protection, not by explicit safe error handling, implying unsafe writes before detection.
JNI entrypoint jdecrypt is a realistic cross-layer path for remote or external data to reach this parser in production use.
recommendations: Audit and rewrite Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage to use strict length-checked parsing and safe APIs (e.g., bounded memcpy/memmove, memcmp) with explicit bounds on all stack buffers.
Add comprehensive ASN.1 input validation (size, nesting depth, length fields) at the earliest possible stage, ideally before calling into Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage.
Compile libUbikeyJni.so with full hardening (stack protector-strong, PIE, RELRO, fortify) and add targeted fuzzing/ASAN builds for all ASN.1 and certificate-related routines.
assumptions: UbikeyJni.jdecrypt ultimately receives data that can be directly or indirectly controlled by an attacker (network, file, NFC, etc.).
Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage operates on data derived from that input without complete length validation for all intermediate buffers.
There is no intermediate Java-side validation strong enough to prevent malformed ASN.1 from reaching the native parser.
limitations: Exact C code and specific buffer/length variables in Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage were not available for direct inspection.

The exact Java signature and argument handling in UbikeyJni.jdecrypt were not decompiled, so the degree of direct attacker control over length parameters is inferred from standard JNI crypto patterns.
No full native call stack beyond the top frames was provided; intermediate helpers, if any, could add checks that are not visible here.

chain_of_thought: Native crash occurs in __memcpy, called from Decode_DER_OctetString inside libUbikeyJni.so, along a chain of ASN.1/DER parsing and certificate decryption routines. This pattern strongly suggests an out-of-bounds read/write or overlap misuse while decoding an attacker-controlled DER octet string.

Tools could not decompile the binary due to HTTP 404 errors, so exact C code and data-flow for src/dst/len in memcpy cannot be shown. However, we can still reason from the symbol names, stack, and context (DER ASN.1 parsing in a crypto-related library exposed to Java via JNI).

Decode_DER_OctetString likely takes a pointer + length for an ASN.1-encoded blob and parses an OCTET STRING: it needs to read the length from the DER header, validate that the claimed length fits within the provided buffer, then memcpy the payload into a destination buffer. The crash in __memcpy implies that the length used is inconsistent with the actual allocation, i.e., either (a) declared length > available input, leading to an out-of-bounds read from src, or (b) destination buffer smaller than the requested length, leading to an out-of-bounds write.

Given the function names Get_DER_Child and Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage, the code seems to construct complex nested structures from the DER, probably assuming well-formed cert exchange messages. A fuzzer causing memcpy to crash has found a path where Decode_DER_OctetString uses unchecked or insufficiently checked lengths, since hardened allocators generally only abort on real over-/underflow, not on benign edge cases.

The JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt suggests this decryption is reachable from Java with attacker-influenced data (e.g., server-provided certificate exchange, bill data from network, or QR code). Fuzz harness entry fuzz_one_input mimics arbitrary input from Java into this JNI method. Unless there is strong pre-validation in Java, arbitrary \u0000 bytes, length fields, etc. will reach the native DER parsing code. No evidence suggests Java constrains DER lengths to a safe bound; without code, the safer assumption is that Java passes a byte[] (or similar) directly.

The crash being in generic memcpy, with no sanitizer frames before it, supports a genuine memory safety issue (CWE-787/Out-of-bounds Write or CWE-125/Out-of-bounds Read). Even if it is an out-of-bounds read rather than write, it still qualifies as a vulnerability compromising memory safety and potentially leaking adjacent memory or enabling information disclosure. If it's a write, impact could extend to code execution depending on heap layout and control data overwritten.

There is no indication that the crash is due to an artificial harness behaviour like passing NULL pointers or impossible sizes unlikely to occur in production. The fuzz harness is simply feeding arbitrary buffers through the JNI entry, which is analogous to untrusted network or file input in the real app. ASN.1/DER decoders are historically fragile and vulnerable, and using memcpy for length-controlled copies without robust bounds checks is a very common source of exploitable flaws.

Because of missing decompiled code, we cannot conclusively label exactly which argument (src vs dst vs len) is untrusted or whether the OOB is read or write. Nonetheless, the evidence of a memcpy crash located in a DER decoding routine reachable from JNI with attacker-controllable input is strong enough to treat this as a likely, security-relevant memory corruption or read bug rather than a benign abort or logic-only failure.

Severity is at least high: memory corruption potential in a decryption routine processing externally supplied messages is typically considered high-impact, with realistic pathways for remote attackers under some conditions (e.g., malicious server / MITM tampering with cert exchange, or manipulated bill documents). Exploitability may require some heap grooming and knowledge of platform, so I classify exploitability as theoretical rather than already proven practical.

Given all of this, I classify this crash as a likely genuine vulnerability in the DER / ASN.1 decoding implementation around Decode_DER_OctetString that misuses memcpy with attacker-controlled length derived from encoded length fields.
reasons: Crash in __memcpy within Decode_DER_OctetString indicates misuse of memcpy with inconsistent length or buffers during DER octet string parsing.
Decode_DER_OctetString and surrounding ASN.1 functions operate on externally supplied certificate exchange messages passed via JNI, so length and content are attacker-influenced.
No evidence of defensive checks or sanitizer-only abort; pattern is consistent with out-of-bounds read/write from malformed DER length fields.
Fuzz harness simply replaces realistic attacker-controlled inputs, not unrealistic JNI misuse (no indication of forced NULLs or impossible arguments).
recommendations: Review and harden Decode_DER_OctetString and related ASN.1/DER parsing routines: ensure all length fields are validated against the actual input buffer size before memcpy/memmove.

Replace raw memcpy with a safe wrapper that enforces explicit bounds checks (e.g., check that src and dst regions are within allocated buffers and that len does not overflow) and add robust error handling for malformed DER.
Add fuzz tests and ASan/UBSan builds for the DER decoding and DecryptCertExchange code paths, using structured ASN.1 fuzzers to confirm no remaining OOB reads/writes under malformed inputs.
assumptions: JNI method Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt accepts attacker-controlled byte data (e.g., from network, file, or IPC).
Java layer does not fully sanitize or normalize the ASN.1 structures before passing them to native code.
The crash in __memcpy arises from invalid length/buffer parameters rather than a hardware fault or unrelated allocator bug.
limitations: Could not decompile libUbikeyJni.so due to tooling 404, so memcpy call sites and exact buffer handling are inferred from symbol names and stack trace.
Exact nature of memory bug (OOB read vs OOB write) is unknown without code or crash register state.

exploit_pipeline: Craft a DER-encoded certificate exchange message whose OCTET STRING length fields are inconsistent with the actual payload size to cause oversized memcpy operations in Decode_DER_OctetString.

Deliver this crafted message to the app via the channel that causes UbikeyJni.jdecrypt to be invoked (e.g., upload a manipulated bill file or intercept/modify TLS-like exchange if this is used for secure messaging).

When the app calls the JNI method, the malformed DER passes into DecryptCertExchange -> Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage -> Get_DER_Child -> Decode_DER_OctetString, triggering an out-of-bounds memcpy and crashing the process or corrupting heap state.
Refine the payload to control overwrite patterns (if it is an OOB write) to achieve information disclosure or, under favorable conditions, code execution.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame is __memcpy, next is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST in libUbikeyJni.so, indicating a memcpy-related memory corruption during DER/ASN.1 decoding of certificate exchange data.

The stack shows a clear app-level function Decode_DER_ASN1_ST calling memcpy, followed by DecryptCertExchange and the JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt, so the fault is in app crypto/parsing code, not harness glue or allocator internals.

DER ASN.1 decoding typically involves variable-length fields driven by length bytes inside the attacker-supplied buffer; if lengths are not thoroughly validated, memcpy can be called with an out-of-bounds source or destination, leading to heap/stack corruption or crashes.

The fuzz harness entry fuzz_one_input feeds data into the JNI decrypt method, effectively standing in for attacker-controlled inputs such as encrypted messages or certificates; thus, the data that drives Decode_DER_ASN1_ST is attacker-controllable in realistic use (remote or untrusted certificates).

__memcpy crash strongly suggests that the length argument or one of the pointers is invalid; in such parsers, this generally arises from trusting internal ASN.1 length fields without complete bounds checking rather than from pure logic aborts.

Because we lack decompiled code, we cannot show the exact memcpy callsite, but the presence of a dedicated Decode_DER_ASN1_ST function and its position right above memcpy heavily imply a manual decoder implementation rather than a hardened library with internal checks.

The Java bridge jdecrypt is a general-purpose decryption function; it is very likely reachable with untrusted ciphertexts or protocol blobs in the production app (smart billing/Ubikey context), so a malformed payload could realistically trigger the same condition outside the fuzz harness.

Given this, the most plausible explanation is an out-of-bounds read/write or buffer overrun in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST (or DecryptCertExchange) due to insufficient validation of attacker-influenced length fields before calling memcpy.

Impact is at least high: incorrect memcpy arguments can corrupt memory, potentially enabling code execution depending on surrounding mitigations; at a minimum it yields a reliable denial-of-service against the app when parsing crafted inputs.

We cannot map stack addresses or show precise dataflow from the JNI parameters to the memcpy arguments, so confidence is slightly below 0.9 but still strong based on function naming, crash site, and typical ASN.1 decoder bug patterns.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy invoked from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST inside app-owned libUbikeyJni.so, indicating unsafe copying in ASN.1/DER decoding logic.
Input data flowing through Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt to DecryptCertExchange and Decode_DER_ASN1_ST is attacker-controllable in realistic scenarios (encrypted messages/certificates).
Manual ASN.1/DER decoders are prone to length/offset mishandling; a memcpy-based crash is best explained by out-of-bounds read/write due to insufficient length validation.
The fault is clearly in application parsing/crypto code rather than in the fuzz harness alone; function names match exported JNI and native symbols in libUbikeyJni.so.
recommendations: Review Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and DecryptCertExchange implementations to ensure all ASN.1 length fields and offsets are strictly bounds-checked against the actual buffer size before any memcpy or similar operations.
Replace ad hoc DER/ASN.1 parsing with a well-tested library where possible, or add comprehensive unit/fuzz tests around certificate and message decoding routines.
Enable and run with address sanitizers and hardened allocators in test builds to localize the exact failing memcpy callsite and confirm the specific out-of-bounds condition.
assumptions: Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt is reachable with untrusted or remote data in the real app, not just via the fuzz harness.

The __memcpy crash is due to invalid length/pointer arguments originating from the parsed input rather than an unrelated memory management bug elsewhere.

prerequisites: Attacker can cause the application to invoke UbikeyJni.jdecrypt with attacker-controlled byte arrays (e.g., as part of a smart billing or authentication workflow).
Native library libUbikeyJni.so is present and used in the deployed app with the vulnerable Decode_DER_ASN1_ST implementation.
Standard platform mitigations (ASLR, stack canaries) may limit straightforward RCE but do not prevent memory corruption/DoS.
exploit_pipeline: Craft a DER/ASN.1-encoded payload with inconsistent or extreme length fields so that Decode_DER_ASN1_ST computes an out-of-bounds region and passes it to memcpy.
Deliver this payload to the application through the protocol or storage mechanism that ultimately calls UbikeyJni.jdecrypt (e.g., network response, QR code, file input).
Trigger the app functionality that invokes UbikeyJni.jdecrypt on the crafted data, causing DecryptCertExchange to call Decode_DER_ASN1_ST.

When Decode_DER_ASN1_ST executes memcpy with attacker-influenced length/pointers, memory is corrupted or an invalid address is accessed, crashing the app and potentially enabling further exploitation depending on layout and surrounding code.
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n com.skt.smartbill/.MainActivity --es payload_path "/sdcard/Download/ubikey_payload.bin"
# Within an instrumented test or PoC harness: invoke UbikeyJni.jdecrypt with the contents of /sdcard/Download/ubikey_payload.bin as the ciphertext/certificate argument.
exploit.notes: Precise exploitation (beyond DoS) would require analyzing the Decode_DER_ASN1_ST implementation and surrounding memory layout to identify controllable heap/stack overflows or metadata corruption; current evidence robustly supports at least a crash-inducing out-of-bounds memcpy driven by input-controlled ASN.1 lengths.
